metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan's autolander deliverables against ADR-0047: the auto-lander (`infra/autolander/`, `Dockerfile.autolander`) was retired and deleted; update the plan to remove autolander-specific steps and AC #2, or explain how signing attribution is now achieved via the replacement Submit-when-green flow.
findings.finding: The plan targets `infra/autolander/loop.py` and `infra/Dockerfile.autolander` as delivery artifacts, but ADR-0047 (accepted 2026-07-13) explicitly retired and deleted the auto-lander: 'Delete infra/autolander/' is listed as migration step 2. Neither file exists in the repo. The plan's second major deliverable is a phantom target, so implementing it as written would produce dead/unreachable code or fail immediately on missing files.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Configure `identity.signing_key` … in both the reviewbot and autolander containers (`infra/compose`, `infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot`, `infra/Dockerfile.autolander`)' and AC #2 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot.'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the autolander container from scope (Dockerfile.autolander, loop.py reference, AC #2). If bot attribution for merge-related comments is still needed, identify the actual current code path (e.g., a Gerrit hook or the reviewbot itself) that posts those comments and target that instead.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify that the signing-key materialization requires a NEW `fetch-secrets.sh` step that writes the key to a named host file (e.g., `/var/rebar/bot-signing.key`, 0600) and bind-mounts it into the container — not a `.env` env-var line — and name the mount path so `identity.signing_key` in the container config can be set to a concrete, resolvable path.
findings.finding: The plan describes extending `fetch-secrets.sh` to 'materialize [the signing key] to a 0600 file (same pattern as GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)' but the existing pattern for those secrets writes values into the `.env` file as env vars — not to individual 0600 files. `identity.signing_key` expects a filesystem path to the key file, so a new materialize-to-file step (analogous to `materialize-deploy-key.sh`) is required, not a `.env` line. The mount path into the container is unspecified, making the `identity.signing_key` config value (a path) impossible to determine from the plan.
findings.location: Plan body — 'extend infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh to materialize it to a 0600 file (same pattern as GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ADR-0008)'; AC #1
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `materialize-bot-signing-key.sh` step (mirroring `materialize-deploy-key.sh`) that writes `/var/rebar/bot-signing.key` (0600) from SSM, mount it read-only into the reviewbot container at a fixed path (e.g., `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing.key`), and set `identity.signing_key = "/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing.key"` in the container's rebar.toml override or via `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify a concrete key-file materialization mechanism (distinct from the .env env-var pattern) — e.g. a script analogous to `infra/gerrit/materialize-deploy-key.sh` that writes the SSM value to a dedicated `0600` file and bind-mounts it into both containers — and update fetch-secrets.sh / Dockerfile / compose accordingly.
findings.finding: The plan says fetch-secrets.sh should materialize the signing key to a '0600 file (same pattern as GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY),' but those secrets are written as KEY=VALUE env-var lines into infra/compose/.env. identity.signing_key requires a FILE PATH to an SSH private key on disk — a plain env-var line like REBAR_BOT_SIGNING_KEY=<raw-key-bytes> cannot be passed directly as a file-path config value. The plan conflates two different materialization modes, leaving the actual 0600 key-file creation step unspecified.
findings.location: Plan body: 'extend infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh to materialize it to a 0600 file (same pattern as GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN/ANTHROPIC_API_KEY, ADR-0008)'; infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify whether the auto-lander (infra/autolander/loop.py) still exists before scoping signing work against it; ADR-0047 retired the entire auto-lander — reconcile the plan with the current repo state or drop the auto-lander AC.
findings.finding: The plan's central premise — 'the auto-lander comments on WORK tickets (non-exempt, infra/autolander/loop.py:310) so it MUST sign' — rests on a file that no longer exists. ADR-0047 (status: Accepted, 2026-07-13) explicitly retired and deleted the entire auto-lander ('Drop the auto-lander entirely. Delete infra/autolander/...'). The directory infra/autolander/ is absent from the repo. The committed work item ('Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot') targets code that has been deleted.
findings.location: Plan body: 'the auto-lander comments on WORK tickets (non-exempt, infra/autolander/loop.py:310)'; AC: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot'
findings.suggested_fix: Remove the auto-lander signing deliverable entirely (it targets deleted code) and update the plan to scope only the review-bot attribution work, which is grounded in existing code.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove references to `infra/autolander/loop.py` and `infra/Dockerfile.autolander` (both deleted by ADR-0047/epic d4de); clarify whether the auto-lander signing scope is deferred, reassigned to a replacement component, or was included in error.
findings.finding: infra/autolander/loop.py (cited at line 310) and infra/Dockerfile.autolander are hallucinated edit targets: the auto-lander was retired and its directory deleted per ADR-0047 (accepted 2026-07-13); neither file exists in the repo.
findings.location: Plan body: 'the auto-lander comments on WORK tickets (non-exempt, `infra/autolander/loop.py:310`)' and 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan with ADR-0047: either confirm the autolander is still deployed (and cite where its Dockerfile/compose config lives), or remove 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander' from scope and revise AC2 to reflect that the autolander is retired.
findings.finding: The plan lists 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander' as a target for bot-identity configuration, but ADR-0047 (Accepted, 2026-07-13) retired the autolander and its code deletion is explicitly planned under stories d0e5/bcf0. The file does not exist in the repository. The plan references a non-existent infra artifact, making AC2 undeliverable as written.
findings.location: Plan body: 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander'; docs/adr/0047-retire-autolander-rebase-if-necessary.md

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Remove all references to `infra/autolander/loop.py`, `infra/Dockerfile.autolander`, and the autolander container from the plan and ACs — the auto-lander was retired per ADR-0047; revise AC #2 to reflect which currently-deployed service (if any) now posts merge-comments on WORK tickets, or drop AC #2 if no such service exists.
findings.finding: The plan cites `infra/autolander/loop.py:310` and `infra/Dockerfile.autolander` as concrete edit targets for wiring the signing identity, but these files do not exist: the auto-lander was fully retired and its directory deleted per ADR-0047 (accepted 2026-07-13, status Accepted). There is no autolander container to configure.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Configure identity.signing_key ... in both the reviewbot and autolander containers (infra/compose, infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot, infra/Dockerfile.autolander)'; also 'auto-lander comments on WORK tickets (non-exempt, infra/autolander/loop.py:310)'
findings.suggested_fix: Audit which service (if any) currently posts comments on WORK ticket merges after the autolander retirement. If it is a Gerrit hook or the review-bot, target that component. If no service fills this role, remove AC #2 entirely and scope the plan to review-bot attribution only.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the plan with ADR-0047: remove or replace references to the deleted `infra/autolander/loop.py` and `infra/Dockerfile.autolander`, and re-scope AC2 to whatever system (if any) now produces merge-comments on WORK tickets.
findings.finding: The plan references `infra/autolander/loop.py:310` and `infra/Dockerfile.autolander` as delivery targets, but neither file nor the `infra/autolander/` directory exists in the repository. ADR-0047 retired and deleted the auto-lander. The plan's rationale ('the auto-lander comments on WORK tickets … so it MUST sign') and its implementation steps for the autolander container are built on non-existent artifacts — the referenced agents/skills do not exist.
findings.location: Plan body: 'infra/autolander/loop.py:310', 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander'; AC2: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Dockerfile path from `infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot` to `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` throughout the plan.
findings.finding: The plan names `infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot` as an edit target, but the file actually lives at `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`. The wrong path could cause confusion during implementation.
findings.location: Plan body: 'infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace `infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot` with `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot` in the plan body.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the Dockerfile paths in the plan: the reviewbot Dockerfile is at `infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot`; `infra/Dockerfile.autolander` does not exist and should be removed.
findings.finding: The plan cites 'infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot' and 'infra/Dockerfile.autolander' as files to be modified, but neither path exists. The reviewbot Dockerfile is at 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot'; there is no Dockerfile.autolander anywhere in the repo.
findings.location: Plan body: 'both the reviewbot and autolander containers (infra/compose, infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot, infra/Dockerfile.autolander)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'infra/Dockerfile.reviewbot, infra/Dockerfile.autolander' with 'infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot' (and drop the autolander reference given finding #1).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify which signing/identity mechanism actually controls Gerrit comment attribution (likely `BOT_USER` / `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN` account identity, not `identity.signing_key`), and replace the `identity.signing_key` configuration step with the correct mechanism; if HMAC manifest signing is intended, use `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` (env var, raw string) per `signing.py`.
findings.finding: `identity.signing_key` in the codebase is specifically the path to an **OpenSSH private key** for DSSE/sshsig **authorship attestation of ticket-store events** (the asymmetric epic gnu-whale-ichor feature, `_seam.py:285`, `attest/authorship.py`). The plan uses this config key name to describe 'bot signing identity' for Gerrit comment attribution — a different mechanism. Setting `identity.signing_key` on the reviewbot container would trigger SSH-key-based event signing in the ticket store, not comment attribution. The HMAC manifest-signing mechanism (`signing.py`) uses `REBAR_SIGNING_KEY` (a raw string), not `identity.signing_key`. The plan's named config key does not wire to the stated goal.
findings.location: Plan body — 'Configure `identity.signing_key` (to that file) + bot identity (git email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) in both the reviewbot and autolander containers'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the exact observable artifact for 'signed as Rebar Bot' in ACs 2 and 3 (e.g., 'Jira comment author display name is "Rebar Bot"' OR 'comment body contains a `-- Rebar Bot` footer line') so the criterion is unambiguously pass/fail.
findings.finding: ACs 2 and 3 state an observable outcome ('signed as Rebar Bot', 'attribute to Rebar Bot') without specifying the exact observable artifact — e.g., a display name in the Jira comment, a git commit author field, or a signature line appended to the comment body — making them subjectively evaluable.
findings.location: AC line: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot' and 'Review-bot writes attribute to Rebar Bot (exempt, but attributed)'
findings.suggested_fix: Example: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on WORK tickets show author display name "Rebar Bot" in Jira (verified by fetching the comment via Jira REST API and asserting `$.author.displayName == "Rebar Bot"`).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify explicitly that `identity.signing_key` should be wired via the `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` environment variable (set to the **file path** of the materialized 0600 key file, not the key bytes) in the container `.env`, consistent with the fetch-secrets.sh pattern.
findings.finding: The plan's `identity.signing_key` configuration mechanism is unspecified: it says 'Configure identity.signing_key (to that file)' but never states whether this is done via the `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY` environment variable or a `rebar.toml` / `pyproject.toml` entry. A committed `rebar.toml` cannot hold a runtime-materialized, per-boot temp-file path, so the two mechanisms are not interchangeable — an implementer who writes a static path into `rebar.toml` will break on every boot, while one who uses the env var achieves the right behavior. The processing protocol (which config seam to use) is not co-located with the referenced config key.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Configure identity.signing_key (to that file)'; src/rebar/_config_schema.py:314 (env override: REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY)
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh, add a step that writes the SSM value to a 0600 key file (e.g., `/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key`) and emits `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=/run/secrets/rebar-bot-signing-key` into the .env — making the mechanism explicit and consistent with the existing pattern.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the exact config mechanism for `identity.signing_key` (e.g., YAML config file path/key, env var name, or git config entry) and the corresponding config-consumption call site in voter.py / loop.py.
findings.finding: The plan references `identity.signing_key` as a config key to set in both containers but does not specify the config mechanism (git config, YAML file, environment variable, or another format), leaving the executor unable to implement it without reading existing code.
findings.location: Body: 'Configure `identity.signing_key` (to that file) + bot identity (git email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`) in both the reviewbot and autolander containers'

findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a key-rotation procedure to the plan: specify whether containers must be restarted, or whether a watch/re-fetch mechanism is needed, so the UPDATE lifecycle leg is covered.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no key-rotation UPDATE procedure: when the signing key is rotated in SSM, the materialized file in running containers becomes stale, and neither bot will re-fetch without an explicit restart or reload trigger.
findings.location: AC: 'fetch-secrets.sh materializes it 0600'; plan body mentions one-time materialization at startup

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether `infra/terraform/user_data.sh` needs updating for the new SSM param, or confirm that fetch-secrets.sh alone (running before compose-up) is sufficient and explain why.
findings.finding: infra/terraform/user_data.sh maintains its own SSM-param-to-env-var map (the PARAMS declare -A) that is separate from fetch-secrets.sh. If the new signing-key param needs to be available at cloud-init time (e.g. to seed the file before containers start), user_data.sh must also be updated — but it is not listed as an edit target.
findings.location: Plan body: 'extend `infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh` to materialize it to a `0600` file'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify that `openssh-client` (>= 8.9) is explicitly installed in both container images (Dockerfile.reviewbot and Dockerfile.autolander), or document how `ssh-keygen` is otherwise available at the version the SSHSIG scheme requires (`SSHSIG_MIN_VERSION = (8, 9)` in `sshsig.py`).
findings.finding: The plan asserts that signing will work in both containers by configuring identity.signing_key, but the signing path (sign_event_authorship → sshsig.sign → ssh-keygen -Y sign) requires openssh-client >= 8.9 in the container. Dockerfile.reviewbot only installs git, curl, and ca-certificates; openssh-client is not explicitly installed. If ssh-keygen is absent, sshsig.ensure_available() raises SshKeygenUnavailable, signing fails silently as best-effort (the event is written unsigned), and AC2 ('auto-lander merge-comments are signed as Rebar Bot') is silently unmet with no startup-time error.
findings.location: Plan body ('Configure identity.signing_key … in both the reviewbot and autolander containers') / infra/compose/Dockerfile.reviewbot (apt-get install line) / src/rebar/attest/sshsig.py:36
findings.suggested_fix: Add `openssh-client` to the `apt-get install` line in both Dockerfiles, and add a build-time smoke-test (`RUN ssh-keygen -Y sign --help || true && ssh -V`) to surface version mismatches at image build time rather than silently at runtime.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one concrete proving command per AC (e.g., a docker compose run that triggers a dry-run comment and `grep`s the output for 'Rebar Bot', or a script that calls the signing path and asserts the authored identity).
findings.finding: None of the three acceptance criteria include a concrete proving command or end-to-end check; ACs 2 and 3 require live exercise of the new signing code path in production (auto-lander commenting, review-bot attributing) but no integration test, CLI invocation, or test-mode trigger is described.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria section — all three ACs

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a criterion (or extend AC #2/#3) that explicitly verifies the git email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` is set in both container configurations, not just the display name.
findings.finding: The plan body describes configuring the git email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` as a named deliverable in both containers, but no acceptance criterion verifies this identity field — ACs #2 and #3 only check that comments are 'signed as Rebar Bot' / 'attribute to Rebar Bot', which could pass even if the git email is misconfigured or omitted.
findings.location: Plan body (identity paragraph) vs. AC #2 and AC #3
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC #2 to read: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot (git email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com); verify via `git log` or comment metadata in the target Gerrit/JIRA ticket.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or update ADR-0008) documenting the Rebar Bot identity contract: canonical email, signing-key SSM path, and the convention for wiring it into new service containers.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new 'bot signing identity' pattern (git email + signing key wired into two service containers) with no ADR or doc update called out. ADR-0008 covers secret materialization but not the architectural decision of a shared bot identity across services.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Configure identity.signing_key … + bot identity (git email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com) in both the reviewbot and autolander containers'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a brief ADR section (or an ADR-0008 addendum) titled 'Bot signing identity' that records: (1) the canonical bot email, (2) the SSM key path convention `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key`, (3) the Dockerfile/compose wiring pattern, and (4) the rotation procedure.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Make the bot git email configurable (e.g., a `REBAR_BOT_EMAIL` env var in compose/Dockerfile) rather than hardcoding `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` in image or source files.
findings.finding: The bot git email `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` is hardcoded in the plan with no indication it will be configurable (e.g., env var or config file entry). This is a value likely to evolve (personnel change, org policy), and if baked into Dockerfiles or source it becomes a maintenance burden.
findings.location: Plan body: 'git email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com'
findings.suggested_fix: In both Dockerfiles set `ENV REBAR_BOT_EMAIL=joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` as a defaulted build arg or runtime env var, then reference it in the identity configuration step, so it can be overridden per-environment without rebuilding.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the CREATE owner and mechanism for `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` (e.g., Terraform `aws_ssm_parameter` resource, or a runbook step) so provisioning is reproducible across environments.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify who creates (provisions) the SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` or when — manual ops, Terraform, or another mechanism — leaving the CREATE leg of the lifecycle undefined.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Add SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a Terraform `aws_ssm_parameter` resource (type = SecureString, key_id = existing KMS key) under `infra/terraform/` and reference it in the plan. If manual, add a one-time runbook step to the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify error-handling behavior for signing key unavailability: fetch-secrets.sh should exit non-zero (blocking container start) if the key cannot be fetched, and loop.py:310 should surface (log + alert) rather than swallow a signing failure.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no error handling for the case where SSM is unavailable at container start-time or the materialized key file is missing/corrupt at runtime when the auto-lander tries to sign (loop.py:310). Given the plan states the auto-lander MUST sign, a silent failure is a reliability gap.
findings.location: AC: 'Auto-lander merge-comments on work tickets are signed as Rebar Bot'; plan body: 'it MUST sign'
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh, add `set -e` guard and explicit exit code on SSM fetch failure. In loop.py, wrap the signing call in a try/except that logs the error, increments a metric counter, and either aborts the comment write or raises so the loop can handle it — do not silently continue unsigned.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an observability requirement: at minimum, log a structured error (including the SSM parameter name and error message) when the signing key cannot be fetched or applied, and surface a metric/alert if the auto-lander loop encounters a signing error.
findings.finding: No observability is specified for signing failures or SSM fetch errors: operators have no metric, log line, or alert to detect when Rebar Bot attribution is broken.
findings.location: Plan body and Acceptance Criteria — no instrumentation mentioned

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one automated test (e.g., a shell/bats test for `fetch-secrets.sh` verifying 0600 materialization and missing-key failure, plus an integration/smoke AC that exercises the live SSM fetch → signing path in a non-prod environment) so the newly-defaulted signing path cannot silently break.
findings.finding: The plan introduces new user-facing behavior (auto-lander signs merge-comments on WORK tickets; review-bot attribution changes) but contains zero test strategy — no unit, integration, or end-to-end tests are mentioned. The ACs are phrased as manual spot-checks. Additionally, the newly-defaulted path (SSM key materialized → signing invoked at runtime) could go green in CI via offline/stub coverage that never exercises the live SSM fetch + file-permission + signing chain, leaving the defaulted path broken in production.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all three ACs) / infra/autolander/loop.py:310
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ACs: (a) a shell test (`bats` or equivalent) that stubs SSM and asserts `fetch-secrets.sh` writes the file at mode 0600 AND exits non-zero when the parameter is absent; (b) an end-to-end smoke test (staging/non-prod) that runs the auto-lander container with a real (test) SSM key and asserts the resulting comment body is signed as `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com`; (c) a unit test in `loop.py` / `voter.py` that asserts the signing identity is applied when `identity.signing_key` is set, and logs an error or raises when the key file is missing.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm whether REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY (the existing env override for identity.signing_key, per _config_schema.py:314) added to the .env via fetch-secrets.sh satisfies the delivery requirement, avoiding a new 0600 key-file materialisation step.
findings.finding: The plan proposes materializing the signing key to a separate 0600 file (similar to materialize-deploy-key.sh), but the codebase already supports REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY as an env-var override for identity.signing_key (src/rebar/_config_schema.py:314). Injecting the key via the existing .env (already written atomically at 0600 by fetch-secrets.sh) and REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY would reuse the established delivery path without a new file-materialization step, avoiding partial duplication of the materialize-deploy-key.sh / materialize-g2p-config.sh pattern.
findings.location: Plan body: 'materialize it to a `0600` file (same pattern as `GERRIT_BOT_TOKEN`/`ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`)'
findings.suggested_fix: In fetch-secrets.sh, add `rebar_bot_signing_key="$(get_param rebar-bot-signing-key)"` and emit `REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY=${rebar_bot_signing_key}` into the .env, then remove the separate 0600 file step and any associated Dockerfile/compose mount. Configure identity.signing_key via the env override rather than a file path.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix the voter.py line-number citation: the exempt-type exemption logic is in `src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:243` (`_AUTHORSHIP_GATE_EXEMPT_TYPES`), not voter.py:241,278; update the plan to cite the correct file and confirm code_review is in that list.
findings.finding: The plan claims 'the review-bot writes only exempt types (review_bot/voter.py:241,278)' — but those line numbers do not contain exempt-type logic. Line 241 of voter.py is inside the review_and_vote function (change_id assignment) and line 278 is a try: block in the ticket-link loop. The exempt-type check that the plan alludes to lives in _seam.py:243 (_AUTHORSHIP_GATE_EXEMPT_TYPES). The assertion is not wrong in spirit (voter.py writes code_review artifacts, which are exempt) but the cited evidence is incorrect, making the claim unverifiable at the stated location.
findings.location: Plan body: 'the review-bot writes only exempt types (review_bot/voter.py:241,278)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'review_bot/voter.py:241,278' with 'src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:243 (_AUTHORSHIP_GATE_EXEMPT_TYPES includes "code_review")'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` to the plan's edit-target list and specify that `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` must be appended to the `rebar_secret_params` local.
findings.finding: infra/terraform/ssm.tf (the Terraform resource that provisions /rebar/prod/* SecureString placeholders) is not listed as an edit target, but adding /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key requires inserting it into the rebar_secret_params local list there.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Add SSM SecureString `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` alongside the other SecureString slots.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly specify that `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` must be added to `rebar_secret_params` in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf` (matching the existing `for_each` pattern with `lifecycle { ignore_changes = [value] }`) so the param is IaC-managed and reproducible.
findings.finding: The plan says 'Add SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key' but never states whether this is done via Terraform (adding the param to rebar_secret_params in infra/terraform/ssm.tf) or via an out-of-band manual aws ssm put-parameter call. The entire existing SSM parameter estate is managed in ssm.tf via for_each; a silent omission here would mean the parameter exists in AWS but not in IaC, creating an untracked resource that drifts from state and won't be recreated on a fresh apply.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Add SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key'; infra/terraform/ssm.tf (rebar_secret_params list)
findings.suggested_fix: Add `"/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key"` to the `rebar_secret_params` list in `infra/terraform/ssm.tf`, consistent with all other SecureString slots. Document in the plan that the Terraform apply creates the placeholder and an operator must overwrite it before boot.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a plan step to create a Rebar Bot `identity` ticket in the tracker (or confirm one exists) and set the identity pointer in each container (via `rebar identity use <id>` at startup or an equivalent `use_identity` API call), so that `resolve_current_identity()` returns non-None and `author_id` is stamped on events.
findings.finding: The plan never specifies that a Rebar Bot `identity` ticket must exist in the tracker and be pointed to via `rebar identity use <id>` (or equivalent). The signing seam at `_seam.py:293-294` conditions signing on BOTH `author_id` resolving AND `signing_key` being set. `resolve_current_identity()` returns None unless either an explicit pointer file (`.rebar/current_identity`) or a git-email match against an *existing* identity ticket is found. Configuring git email + signing key alone is insufficient — without a matching identity ticket, `author_id` is absent, and signing silently does not occur.
findings.location: Plan body: 'Configure identity.signing_key (to that file) + bot identity (git email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com) in both … containers'; src/rebar/_commands/_seam.py:293-294, src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544
findings.suggested_fix: In the container entrypoint (or a one-time bootstrap step), run: `rebar identity create --name 'Rebar Bot' --email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com --self` (idempotent if the ticket already exists), then `rebar identity use <resulting-id>`, so the email pointer resolves at runtime.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
